name: Vodka
cultural_notes: The most versatile spirit. Eastern European vodka culture emphasizes drinking it neat and chilled.
safety_notes: Neutral flavor makes it easy to over-consume when mixed
High ABV (40%+) causes rapid impairment
risk_factors: Alcohol poisoning risk if 'binge-drinking' via shots
recommendations: Always measure pours (use a jigger)
Follow the 'one-to-one' rule: one glass of water for every shot

name: Whiskey
cultural_notes: Aged spirit with regional variations like Scotch and Bourbon.
safety_notes: High ABV; intended for slow consumption
Aged spirits contain congeners which may intensify hangovers
recommendations: Add a few drops of water to open the aroma and slightly lower ABV
Avoid 'shooting' high-quality whiskey

name: Tequila
cultural_notes: Made from blue agave. Quality varies from 'Mixto' to '100% Agave'.
safety_notes: 100% Agave tequilas usually result in 'cleaner' processing by the liver than Mixtos
Rapid shot-taking is the primary risk factor
recommendations: Look for '100% De Agave' on the label to avoid added sugars/syrups
Sip 100% agave tequilas like a fine cognac

name: Aguardiente
cultural_notes: Colombia's national spirit. Often drunk as shots in social gatherings.
safety_notes: Lower ABV than vodka (29%), but consumed in high volume socially
Anise can cause heartburn in some
risk_factors: Sugar content in 'Sin Azúcar' versions is lower but still present
recommendations: Eat 'picada' (fried snacks) while drinking to slow absorption
Set a personal limit before the 'botella' is finished

name: Pastis / Ouzo / Arak
cultural_notes: These spirits turn cloudy (the 'Louche' effect) when water is added.
safety_notes: High ABV (45%+) is mitigated by significant water dilution
Drinking neat is extremely potent and not culturally standard
recommendations: Always dilute with at least 3-5 parts water
Drink alongside salty appetizers (olives, feta, nuts)

name: Baijiu
cultural_notes: The world's most consumed spirit. Essential to Chinese business etiquette.
safety_notes: Extremely high ABV (often 52-65%)
Banquet 'Ganbei' (bottoms up) culture leads to rapid intoxication
recommendations: Eat constantly throughout the banquet
Small sips between mandatory toasts are key for longevity
Check your local transport—never drive after a Baijiu session

name: Soju
cultural_notes: Traditionally distilled; now often diluted. Often mixed with beer (Somaek).
safety_notes: Low cost and approachable flavor lead to high-volume consumption
20% ABV is 'the danger zone'—stronger than wine, smoother than vodka
risk_factors: The 'Soju Creep' (intoxication hits suddenly after several bottles)
recommendations: Always pour for others and let others pour for you to regulate pace
Avoid flavored 'fruity' sojus if you want to avoid a next-day headache

name: Ogogoro / Akpeteshie
cultural_notes: Distilled from palm wine or sugarcane juice. Significant cultural heritage.
safety_notes: Informal production can lead to highly variable ABV levels
High purity can be very harsh on the throat
recommendations: Only consume regulated, branded versions in commercial bottles
Treat as a high-strength sipping spirit

name: Siddiki (Saudi Arabia)
cultural_notes: Home-distilled spirit in Saudi Arabia. Illegal but present in expat and local circles.
legal_notes: Illegal in Saudi Arabia; severe penalties
safety_notes: Legal risk is the primary concern
Home distillation lacks safety standards and filtration
recommendations: Extreme caution is advised due to legal and health risks
Ensure source is highly trusted if encountered

name: Rum
cultural_notes: Made from sugarcane. Ranges from light (Cuban) to dark (Jamaican) with overproof varieties existing.
safety_notes: Overproof rums (60%+) are common and significantly increase fire and intoxication risks
Often used in sugary Tiki drinks that hide alcohol potency
recommendations: Avoid 'Overproof' or '151' rums unless diluted in a large punch
Stick to one Tiki drink per 90 minutes due to high alcohol volume

name: Gin
cultural_notes: Flavored primarily with juniper berries. The craft gin revolution has created hundreds of botanical variations.
safety_notes: High spirit concentration
Botanicals can vary widely; check for personal allergies to specific herbs
recommendations: Mix with high-quality tonic to control ABV through dilution
Excellent for sipping slowly when served as a Gin & Tonic

name: Mezcal
cultural_notes: The smoky ancestor of tequila. Often artisanal and produced in small batches.
safety_notes: Usually higher ABV (45-50%) than standard tequila
Traditionally sipped neat, which requires a slower pace
risk_factors: Higher methanol potential in non-certified artisanal batches
recommendations: Sip, don't shoot; follow the Oaxacan saying: 'Kiss the Mezcal'
Eat orange slices with worm salt (sal de gusano) to cleanse the palate

name: Pisco
cultural_notes: South American grape brandy. Subject of ongoing dispute between Peru and Chile.
safety_notes: High-proof grape distillate; very smooth but potent
Often the base of the 'stealthy' Pisco Sour
risk_factors: Rapid absorption on an empty stomach
recommendations: Switch to a Piscola (Pisco & Cola) if you want a longer, more diluted drink

name: Cachaça
cultural_notes: Brazil's national spirit, made from fresh sugarcane juice. Essential for Caipirinhas.
safety_notes: Potency is often masked by lime and sugar in cocktails
Unaged varieties can be quite harsh
recommendations: Look for 'Envelhecida' (aged) Cachaça for a smoother sipping experience
Limit intake if consuming in high-heat environments

name: Grappa
cultural_notes: Italian pomace brandy. Traditionally served after dinner to aid digestion.
safety_notes: High alcohol concentration is hard on the stomach lining if not following a meal
Very sharp flavor usually self-regulates intake
recommendations: Only consume as a small (1oz) digestif after a substantial meal

name: Rakija
cultural_notes: National drink across the Balkans. Often homemade and central to hospitality.
safety_notes: Homemade versions can reach 60%+ ABV; always ask the host about the strength
Social etiquette often requires accepting a refill
risk_factors: Social pressure to consume multiple shots
recommendations: Sip the drink even if served in a shot glass
Eat 'Meze' (small plates) continuously to buffer the alcohol

name: Shochu
cultural_notes: Distilled spirit from sweet potato, barley, or rice. Often drunk with water.
safety_notes: Lower ABV than vodka or whiskey (25% vs 40%)
Very versatile for dilution
risk_factors: Easy to drink in high volume
recommendations: Drink it 'Mizuwari' (with cold water) or 'Oyuwari' (with hot water) to lower ABV further

name: Araq-e Angur (Afghanistan)
cultural_notes: Traditional Afghan grape spirit, produced despite restrictions.
safety_notes: Legal risk is the primary danger (imprisonment or fines)
Underground production means zero quality control
recommendations: Avoid entirely within Afghanistan due to legal and safety concerns
